title: Preparing Cotton Rags for Papermaking
subtitle: Sorting, washing, cutting, and blending cotton fibre for the vat
excerpt: Cotton rags produce long-fibre pulp with a natural archival quality that cotton linter cannot fully match. The preparation takes more time than using processed linter but rewards it with a sheet of distinct character. This technique covers sorting, washing, optional alkali cooking, cutting down, and blending.
sourceNotes: Hunter, Dard. Old Papermaking. Chillicothe, Ohio: Mountain House Press, 1923. Chapter on fibre preparation.

glossaryTerms.definition: Short fibres remaining on the cottonseed after the long staple fibre has been removed by ginning. Sold pre-processed as sheets or balls for papermaking; produces a smooth, even sheet.
Cellulose fibre suspended in water, ready to be formed into a sheet on the mould. Pulp consistency determines sheet thickness and formation quality.
Partially beaten or blended fibre that has been cooked and washed but not yet brought to final vat consistency. Historic mills stored half-stuff and completed the beating when needed.

attrs.heading: Blender safety
attrs.body: Keep the blender unplugged when loading with fibre. Drain the vat before reaching in. Treat the blender the same way you would any electrical appliance near water.

content.text: Linter versus torn rags
The distinction between
cotton linter

and torn rags is fibre length. Linter fibres are short, typically 2 to 8 mm. They form quickly on the mould, drain fast, and produce a smooth, even sheet. Torn cotton rags contain long staple fibres from the original cloth, sometimes 20 to 40 mm. Long fibres interlock more thoroughly during formation, producing a sheet with better tear resistance, more visible surface texture, and a characteristic roughness that holds sizing and ink differently.

Dard Hunter's 1923 study of traditional mill methods names rag paper as the better product precisely because of this fibre length. For writing paper, printmaking paper, or anything that needs to last years of use, the extra prep time is worth it.
Sorting and selecting rags

Use only 100% cotton. Mixed fibres, especially polyester blends, do not break down into cellulose during blending. They stay as stiff synthetic strands in the finished sheet, visible up close and bad for the paper's long-term stability.

Check garment labels and reject anything with polyester, nylon, lycra, viscose, rayon, or modal. Old 100% cotton shirt fabric, cotton sheeting, cotton muslin, and cotton calico are all suitable. Knitted cotton (jersey) is also usable but produces a slightly different fibre structure than woven cloth.

Remove all non-cellulose elements: buttons, zips, elastic waistbands, seam reinforcement tape, press studs, printed patches, and iron-on transfers. All of these will survive the blender and contaminate the sheet. Cut carefully around any printed motifs; screen-print ink sits on top of the fibre and can be cut away.
Washing

Wash the sorted cloth in hot water with a small amount of washing soda (sodium carbonate), not detergent. Detergent leaves a surfactant residue in the fibre that can affect how the finished sheet accepts sizing and ink. Washing soda dissolves in water and rinses clean.

Two wash cycles are sufficient for clean rags. Heavily soiled material, such as old drop-cloths or workshop rags, should go through three cycles and a long rinse. Coloured cloth will bleed colour in the first wash and should be washed separately to avoid staining white or pale material. Heavily dyed cloth may need a longer soak.
Optional alkali cook

Traditional mills cooked rags in wood-ash lye or lime water before beating. The alkali breaks down non-cellulose plant compounds (sizing, starch, pectin) in the cloth and softens the fibre structure, making subsequent blending faster and more complete.

For a home cook: dissolve one tablespoon of soda ash (sodium carbonate, not bicarbonate) per litre of water in a stainless steel or enamel pot. Submerge the washed rags, bring to a gentle simmer, and hold for 30 to 45 minutes. Do not boil vigorously; this degrades fibre length. After cooking, drain and rinse under cold water until the water runs clear and no longer feels slippery. This intermediate stage is
half-stuff
: fully prepared but not yet blended. It can be squeezed out and stored in a sealed bag in the refrigerator for several days.
Cutting down

Even after washing and cooking, whole pieces of cloth are too large for a domestic blender. Cut the rags into pieces no larger than 2 to 3 cm square. Long strips also work; the blender handles them if they are no wider than 2 cm. Cutting is the most time-consuming step. A rotary cutter and self-healing mat speed this up significantly compared to scissors.
Blending
Fill the blender jar two-thirds full with water. Add a small handful of cut rags, no more than what fits loosely in one palm. Overloading strains the motor and produces unevenly beaten
pulp
with long unbeaten strands alongside finely processed fibre.

Blend in 10-second bursts with a pause between each to let the motor cool. Cotton rags require more blending time than linter: typically 3 to 5 cycles of 10 seconds each to achieve a good formation without over-beating. Over-beating shortens the fibres back toward linter length and sacrifices the advantage of using rags in the first place.

Test by filling a clear glass with water and adding a spoonful of the blended pulp. Swirl and hold to the light. The fibres should be visibly separated with no visible cloth weave remaining, but not reduced to a fine mist. A few fibre clumps that separate when disturbed are acceptable; solid cloth fragments are not.

Transfer each blended batch to the vat as it is finished. When the vat is full, top up with water to the working level (typically 20 to 25 cm deep for an A4 mould) and stir thoroughly before pulling the first sheet.
